did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) combined with handcrafted texture features to perform melanoma detection (anomaly detection) on dermoscopic skin lesion images. The model was trained on the ISIC2018 and ISIC2019 datasets and evaluated on the ISIC2016 dataset to classify melanomas versus nevi.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with injected handcrafted features for melanoma detection. It details specific methodology (training on ISIC2018/2019, testing on ISIC2016 and DermIS), reports quantitative results (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, AUC), and compares the proposed model against pre-trained networks and existing literature, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Anomaly Detection for Skin Lesion Images Using Convolutional Neural Network and Injection of Handcrafted Features: A Method That Bypasses the Preprocessing of Dermoscopic Images
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of distinguishing melanomas from nevi by proposing an anomaly detection method that bypasses traditional image preprocessing and segmentation steps. The authors developed a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) that integrates handcrafted texture features extracted from whole dermoscopic images as additional input during training. The model was trained on the ISIC2018 and ISIC2019 datasets, focusing exclusively on melanoma and nevus classes, and evaluated on the ISIC2016 dataset and the DermIS database. The proposed approach was compared against widely used pre-trained networks (such as ResNet50 and VGG16) and existing literature results. The key result demonstrated that the custom CNN achieved superior performance in terms of accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and AUC, while also requiring significantly less computational cost and execution time than the pre-trained models.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for the anomaly detection task. Images were selected from ISIC2018, ISIC2019, and ISIC2016 datasets where the diagnosis of melanoma was expressed with a score of 1 in the 'MEL' box.

Used as the 'normal condition' or baseline for the anomaly detection task. Images were selected from ISIC2018, ISIC2019, and ISIC2016 datasets where the diagnosis of nevus was expressed with a score of 1 in the 'NV' box. The study explicitly considers only N and M classes for the experiment, despite other classes being present in the source datasets.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into melanoma (malignant) or nevus (benign) classes.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly frames the task as 'melanoma detection' and 'anomaly detection' with respect to nevi, which is implemented as a binary classification problem. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (Melanoma vs. Nevus). Performance is evaluated using classification metrics including accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and AUC. The study bypasses segmentation and preprocessing to directly classify whole dermoscopic images.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: Part of the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) archive, specifically the ISBI challenge 2018 dataset. Contains dermoscopic images with ground truth in CSV format.

use.training: Used for training the custom CNN model. Only N and M classes were selected for the binary classification task. Random downsampling was applied to the N class to balance it with the M class.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: Part of the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) archive, specifically the ISBI challenge 2019 dataset. Contains dermoscopic images with ground truth in CSV format.

use.training: Used for training the custom CNN model. Only N and M classes were selected for the binary classification task. Random downsampling was applied to the N class to balance it with the M class.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2016
datasets_used.description: Part of the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) archive, specifically the dataset intended for testing in the ISBI 2018 competition. Contains dermoscopic images with ground truth.

use.testing: Used for testing the model to provide comparison with literature results. Only N and M classes were selected.

datasets_used.name: DermIS (Dermatology Information System)
datasets_used.description: An online public database containing dermoscopic images. It is described as the largest public dataset after the ISIC archive.

use.testing: Used for external validation to show robustness of the proposed work.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To balance the class distribution between nevi and melanomas in the training datasets (ISIC2018 and ISIC2019) by reducing the number of nevi images to match the number of melanoma images.
To reduce the dimensionality of the LBP texture features to 1/3 and 1/2 of their original sizes for comparison with using all original features.
To increase the number of melanoma images in the training set (scenario DA Mx3) by applying rotation, shifts, shear, zoom, and horizontal flip, to compare against the balanced downsampling approach.
To visualize and compare the performance of different CNN configurations (with and without injected features) in terms of the trade-off between sensitivity and specificity.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the proposed model and others were based on direct metric values (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, AUC) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2016 (Test Dataset).description: Primary testing dataset for comparison with literature and pre-trained models. Only N (Nevi) and M (Melanoma) classes were used.
ISIC 2016 (Test Dataset).notes: The proposed work achieved the highest performance among all compared models and literature results on the ISIC 2016 dataset. Pre-trained networks generally showed lower accuracy and specificity compared to the proposed custom CNN. The internal validation results (Table 4) differ from the final ISIC 2016 test results (Table 9), likely due to different data splits or the specific final evaluation on the full ISIC 2016 test set.

DermIS Database.description: External validation dataset used to show robustness. Contains 500 Benign and 500 Malignant images.

architecture_details.feature_injection: Handcrafted Local Binary Pattern (LBP) texture features concatenated to flattened CNN features

classification_or_output_head.input_to_head: Flattened 2D features concatenated with handcrafted LBP features

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: CNN with injected LBP features (CNN LBP)
CNN with injected GLCM features (CNN GLCM)
CNN with injected GLCM and LBP features (CNN G&L)
preprocessing_techniques: The study explicitly bypasses traditional preprocessing and segmentation steps for the primary proposed model (CNN LBP), using original, unprocessed dermoscopic images scaled to 224x224 pixels.

Experimental comparison of preprocessing techniques included: Gaussian Filter (GF) with standard deviations (σ) of 1, 3, and 5; Histogram Equalization (HE); Color Normalization (CN) based on color constancy; and combinations of GF+HE, GF+CN, and HE+CN.
Random downsampling was applied to the nevi class in the training datasets (ISIC2018 and ISIC2019) to balance class distribution with melanomas.
Principal Component Analysis (PCA) was used to reduce the dimensionality of LBP texture features to 1/3 and 1/2 of their original sizes.
Data augmentation techniques (rotation, shifts, shear, zoom, and horizontal flip) were applied to increase the number of melanoma images in one experimental scenario (DA Mx3).

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the melanoma class in the 'DA Mx3' scenario to triple the number of melanoma images. Specific settings: rotation (range = 20), width shift (range = 0.1), height shift (range = 0.1), shear (range = 0.3), zoom (range = 0.3), horizontal flip, and fill mode = 'nearest'.

Used for class balancing rather than augmentation, applied to the nevi class in the training datasets (ISIC2018 and ISIC2019). The number of nevi images was reduced to match the number of melanoma images (e.g., from 6705 to 2818 in ISIC2018 and from 12,875 to 2818 in ISIC2019).

conclusions: The proposed anomaly detection method, which combines a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with injected handcrafted Local Binary Pattern (LBP) texture features, successfully bypasses traditional preprocessing and segmentation steps while maintaining high classification performance.

The custom CNN model outperformed widely used pre-trained networks (VGG16, ResNet50, MobileNet, DenseNet121/169/201) in terms of accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and AUC on the ISIC 2016 dataset, achieving 98.15% accuracy, 97.33% sensitivity, 98.35% specificity, and 97.84% AUC.

The proposed model demonstrated superior computational efficiency compared to pre-trained networks, requiring significantly fewer trainable parameters (~150,000 vs. millions) and less training time per epoch.

Injecting handcrafted LBP features into the CNN improved classification performance compared to using the CNN without features or with GLCM features, with the combination of GLCM and LBP features not providing additional benefit over LBP alone.

The proposed model achieved high performance on the external DermIS database (81.50% accuracy, 92.71% sensitivity, 71.15% specificity), showing robustness, although it had lower specificity than some literature comparisons, leading to a higher rate of false positives.

The study suggests that the high performance on the ISIC 2016 dataset may be attributed to the use of unpreprocessed whole images rather than segmented lesions, although the exact contribution of bypassing preprocessing versus feature extraction method could not be definitively isolated.

The authors acknowledge that the lack of preprocessing might introduce spurious correlations from image disturbances (e.g., black contours) and suggest that future work should investigate partial elimination of such disturbances to potentially improve results.
important_remarks: The study explicitly bypasses traditional preprocessing and segmentation steps, using original, unprocessed dermoscopic images, which may introduce spurious correlations from image artifacts such as black contours or hairs.

The model was trained exclusively on the ISIC2018 and ISIC2019 datasets and tested on the ISIC2016 dataset and DermIS database; however, the paper acknowledges a lack of real-world clinical validation and notes that dermoscopic datasets predominantly contain images of light-skinned individuals, limiting generalizability to dark-skinned patients.

Class imbalance was addressed via random downsampling of the nevi class rather than data augmentation for the primary model, as alternative balancing methods (no balancing or tripling melanoma images via augmentation) resulted in poor or unstable performance (e.g., zero sensitivity in the DA Mx3 scenario).
The paper does not report any formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals for the performance comparisons; results are based on direct metric values.

The proposed custom CNN (~150,000 parameters) significantly outperformed pre-trained networks (VGG16, ResNet50, MobileNet, DenseNets) in accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and AUC on the ISIC 2016 dataset, while requiring less computational cost and execution time.

The study focuses only on binary classification of melanoma versus nevus, excluding other skin lesion types (e.g., BCC, SK) present in the source datasets, which limits the scope of the anomaly detection task.

The authors note that the high performance on ISIC 2016 might be attributed to the use of unpreprocessed whole images, but the exact contribution of bypassing preprocessing versus the specific feature extraction method could not be definitively isolated.
